41.title: Aladdin and the Magic Lamp

vocabulary.definition: lazy; not wanting to work
vocabulary.sentence: He was not a bad boy, but he was idle — he preferred to play in the streets with his friends rather than learn a trade.

vocabulary.definition: a person who practices magic, especially dark or evil magic
vocabulary.sentence: He was a sorcerer from the far western lands of Africa, and he had traveled thousands of miles because he had read in his magic books of a hidden lamp.

vocabulary.definition: spending too much money; wastefulness
vocabulary.sentence: He did not wish for extravagance. He grew from an idle boy into a thoughtful young man.

comprehension: Why did the sorcerer come looking for Aladdin?
How did Aladdin get trapped in the cave?
What did Aladdin learn about wishes after he had the lamp?
How did the sorcerer trick the princess into giving him the lamp?
41.journal: If you had one wish from the genie, what would you wish for and why? How would you use it wisely?

informational.title: The Real History of Aladdin
informational.part1: Most people think Aladdin is an ancient Arabian tale that has been told for thousands of years, but the truth is more surprising. Aladdin was actually added to 'One Thousand and One Nights' by a French translator named Antoine Galland in the 1700s. Galland claimed that a Syrian storyteller named Hanna Diyab told him the tale in Paris, but no one has ever found an earlier Arabic version of the story. The original collection of Arabian Nights stories did not include Aladdin at all. Galland heard the story orally, wrote it down in French, and published it as part of his translation. It became so popular that people assumed it had always been part of the collection.
informational.part2: The story itself is unusual because it is set in China but includes Middle Eastern details like mosques, sultans, and genies. This mix shows how stories travel and change as they pass from culture to culture. What we know as Aladdin today came from a French scholar's memory of an oral story, which was later translated back into Arabic, and eventually turned into plays, movies, and books around the world. The Disney movie made Aladdin even more famous, but it changed many details from Galland's version. The story's journey from a Syrian storyteller to a French book to a global legend is almost as magical as the lamp itself. Sometimes the history of a story is just as interesting as the story.

41.opinion: Do you think Aladdin was right to take the jewels from the cave even though the sorcerer warned him not to? Explain your thinking.

grammar.example: He was not a bad boy, but he was idle — he preferred to play in the streets with his friends.

42.title: Ali Baba and the Forty Thieves

vocabulary.definition: a person who carries loads for others
vocabulary.sentence: Ali Baba had married a woman as poor as himself and made his living by cutting and selling wood in the forest.

vocabulary.definition: a hidden supply or treasure stored up
vocabulary.sentence: The thieves had been accumulating this hoard for many years.

vocabulary.definition: able to find clever ways to solve problems
vocabulary.sentence: They called upon a clever and resourceful slave woman named Morgiana, who worked in Cassim's household.

comprehension: What words did Ali Baba use to open the cave?
Why did Cassim get trapped inside the cave?
How did Morgiana save Ali Baba's life three times?
What lesson did Ali Baba learn about fortune and courage?
42.journal: Morgiana was brave, clever, and loyal. Write about someone you know (or have read about) who shows these same qualities.

informational.title: The Power of Secret Passwords
informational.part1: 'Open Sesame' is one of the most famous passwords in all of literature. But why did the thieves choose the word 'sesame'? In the original Arabic tales, sesame seeds were extremely valuable. They were used for cooking oil, medicine, and trade. Sesame was also symbolic because the pods pop open suddenly when they are ripe, which might represent the cave door bursting open. The phrase 'Open Sesame' has become so famous that people still use it today when they want something to open magically. It appears in cartoons, movies, and even in the names of children's TV shows. A simple password from an old story became part of world culture.
informational.part2: Today we use passwords everywhere: for phones, computers, bank accounts, email, and apps. A good password should be hard for others to guess but easy for you to remember—just like 'Open Sesame' was memorable for Ali Baba. Computer experts say the worst passwords people use are '123456,' 'password,' and 'qwerty.' These are easy to guess because they are so common. Better passwords use a mix of letters, numbers, and symbols, or they use a phrase that only you would know. The thieves in Ali Baba's story protected their treasure with one secret word. We protect our information with many passwords, and we have to remember them all. In both cases, the lesson is the same: keep your passwords secret, make them strong, and never share them with people you don't trust completely.

42.opinion: Do you think Ali Baba was right to free Morgiana and let her marry his son? Why or why not?

grammar.example: She was quick-thinking and loyal and entirely unafraid.

43.title: Ali Cogia, the Merchant of Baghdad

vocabulary.definition: a religious journey to a holy place
vocabulary.sentence: For many years he had wanted to make the pilgrimage to Mecca — the holy journey that every person of faith hopes to make once in a lifetime.

vocabulary.definition: a decision or judgment, especially in a court of law
vocabulary.sentence: The merchant's verdict was clear: the bottom olives were many years old. The top olives were recent.

vocabulary.definition: special skill or knowledge in a particular subject
vocabulary.sentence: He sent for an olive merchant — a real one, well known for his expertise.

comprehension: Why did Ali Cogia leave his jar with his neighbor?
How did Hagi Hassan discover the gold?
What clever solution did the Cadi learn from the children's game?
How did the olive merchant prove that the jar had been opened?
43.journal: The Cadi learned the solution to a problem by listening to children playing. Write about a time when you learned something important from an unexpected place.

informational.title: How Courts Use Expert Witnesses
informational.part1: In Ali Cogia's story, the judge called an olive merchant to examine the evidence—someone with special knowledge about olives who could tell old ones from new ones. This person is called an expert witness. Courts today still use expert witnesses to help solve difficult cases where special knowledge is needed. A handwriting expert might examine a signature to see if it is real or forged. A doctor might explain an injury and whether it matches the story someone told. A computer specialist might recover deleted files from a hard drive. A fingerprint analyst can compare prints found at a crime scene. These experts don't decide who wins the case—the judge or jury does that—but they provide information that helps everyone understand the facts.
informational.part2: Expert witnesses must be qualified, which means they need education, training, and experience in their field. They take an oath to tell the truth, just like any other witness. Lawyers can ask them questions, and the opposing side can challenge their opinions. In Ali Cogia's case, the olive merchant's expertise was clear and convincing: the bottom olives were years old, and the top olives were fresh. That evidence proved the jar had been opened and refilled. Just like the olive merchant could see what others missed, expert witnesses today help courts understand science, medicine, technology, and many other fields. The legal system depends on people who know their subjects deeply and can explain them clearly. Sometimes the smallest detail—like the age of an olive—can solve the whole case.

43.opinion: Was Hagi Hassan wrong to take the gold, or was it Ali Cogia's fault for being gone for seven years? Explain your view.

grammar.example: He had one problem: he had saved a thousand pieces of gold and he could not take them with him.

44.title: Sinbad and the Valley of Diamonds

vocabulary.definition: a long piece of cloth wound around the head
vocabulary.sentence: I unwound my turban — a long piece of cloth — and tied myself firmly to the Roc's leg.

vocabulary.definition: extremely large
vocabulary.sentence: An immense bird was descending from the sky — a bird so large that when it spread its wings, the sun disappeared.

vocabulary.definition: something accepted as true without proof
vocabulary.sentence: He thought about what he had said on the steps outside — the bitterness of it, the assumption.

comprehension: How did Sinbad get stranded on the island?
What was the white dome that Sinbad found?
How did Sinbad escape from the valley of diamonds?
What did Hindbad learn from hearing Sinbad's story?
44.journal: Sinbad told Hindbad, 'We rarely know the story behind what we see.' Write about what this means and why it's important.

informational.title: The Legend of the Roc
informational.part1: The Roc is a giant mythical bird from Arabian and Persian stories, so enormous that it could carry elephants in its claws. In Sinbad's tale, the Roc's egg was as large as a house. Ancient sailors told stories of the Roc after voyaging to islands in the Indian Ocean where they saw or heard about real giant birds. The elephant bird of Madagascar stood 10 feet tall and laid eggs that could hold two gallons of liquid—bigger than any bird egg in the world today. These real birds went extinct about 1,000 years ago, but sailors' tales kept the legend alive and made the birds even larger in the retelling. The famous traveler Marco Polo wrote about the Roc in his account of his journeys, describing it as a bird so large its wings blocked out the sun.
informational.part2: The Roc appears in many of Sinbad's voyages and in other Middle Eastern tales. Some scholars believe the legend also came from sightings of very large eagles or vultures seen from a distance, which sailors exaggerated after months at sea. Others think the Roc was inspired by fossils of pterosaurs—giant flying reptiles that lived millions of years ago—which people might have found and not understood. Whatever its origin, the Roc became a powerful symbol of nature's overwhelming force. It was dangerous but not evil; it was simply so large and powerful that humans were tiny by comparison. The Roc in Sinbad's story might be an exaggerated memory of real extinct birds, mixed with human imagination and the storytelling tradition that makes every tale a little bigger than the one before.

44.opinion: Hindbad judged Sinbad before knowing his story. Why do people judge others without knowing the full truth?

grammar.example: Then I remembered something (the old sailors sometimes spoke of how merchants collected diamonds).

46.title: The Ebony Horse

vocabulary.definition: a very hard, dark black wood
vocabulary.sentence: The third brought a horse carved entirely from black ebony wood, inlaid with gold and set with jewels.

vocabulary.definition: extremely happy and excited
vocabulary.sentence: He was exhilarated at first — the wind rushing past him, the earth spread out below in a patchwork of brown and green.

vocabulary.definition: in a careful, orderly, step-by-step way
vocabulary.sentence: Firouz Shah worked through the problem methodically, trying not to panic.

comprehension: What special ability did the ebony horse have?
Why did Prince Firouz Shah have trouble landing the horse at first?
How did the Indian craftsman get revenge on the prince?
What lesson did Firouz Shah learn about the flying horse?
46.journal: The prince learned to stay calm and think through problems when he was afraid. Write about a time you had to think clearly in a scary or confusing situation.

informational.title: The Science of Flight: From Dreams to Reality
informational.part1: For thousands of years, humans dreamed of flying like birds—or like Prince Firouz Shah on his magical ebony horse. Ancient Greek myths told of Icarus, who flew with wings made of feathers and wax. Leonardo da Vinci drew detailed sketches of flying machines in the 1400s, including ornithopters that flapped like bird wings. But powered, controlled flight by humans was not achieved until December 17, 1903, when Orville and Wilbur Wright flew their airplane at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina. That first flight lasted only 12 seconds and covered 120 feet—about the length of a basketball court. Just 66 years later, in 1969, humans landed on the moon. The journey from the Wright Brothers' first short hop to landing on another world happened in a single human lifetime.
informational.part2: Today's airplanes use the same basic principles the prince discovered on his ebony horse: controls for going up, controls for going down, and controls for direction. Modern aircraft have elevators (to pitch the nose up or down), ailerons (to roll the wings side to side), and rudders (to turn left or right). Pilots must learn all these controls before they fly—unlike the prince, who jumped on the magical horse and figured it out in mid-air! Modern flight training takes months of study and practice. The ebony horse's three pegs (up, down, and a hidden control inside the mane) match how real aircraft have multiple control systems that must work together. The ancient storytellers who imagined the ebony horse understood something important: flight requires not just getting into the air, but controlling your movement once you are there.

46.opinion: Was the prince foolish to jump on the horse without learning the controls first, or was he brave? Explain.

grammar.example: She believed him by now, and she was curious about the flying horse, and she had fallen in love with him.

47.title: The Fisherman and the Genie

vocabulary.definition: a magical spirit in Arabian folklore
vocabulary.sentence: It was a genie — but not a pleasant one. His face was dark and fierce.

vocabulary.definition: turned sour or bad (like milk that has gone bad)
vocabulary.sentence: By the third hundred years, my gratitude had curdled into bitterness.

vocabulary.definition: to feel sorry for something you did wrong
vocabulary.sentence: Solomon! I repent! I repent! I will never defy again!

comprehension: Why was the genie angry when he was released?
How did the fisherman trick the genie back into the jar?
What did the genie promise in exchange for his freedom?
Why did the fisherman keep the brass jar after the genie was gone?
47.journal: The fisherman couldn't fight the genie, but he could outsmart him. Write about a time when thinking cleverly was more important than being strong.

informational.title: Genies in Middle Eastern Folklore
informational.part1: Genies—called 'jinn' in Arabic—appear throughout Middle Eastern stories, religious texts, and folklore. Unlike the friendly blue genie in Disney's Aladdin, traditional jinn are powerful and complex spirits made of smokeless fire. They were created before humans, according to Islamic tradition, and have free will just like people do. Some jinn are good, some are evil, and many are neutral—they can be helpful or harmful depending on how they are treated. Jinn can be invisible or take any form, including human or animal shapes. They live for thousands of years but are not immortal. People believed jinn lived in deserts, ruins, caves, and dark places, and that disturbing them could be dangerous.
informational.part2: King Solomon was said to have power over jinn and could command them to do great works, like building his temple. Disobedient jinn were punished by being trapped in sealed containers—bronze jars, lamps, or bottles—which is why the genie in 'The Fisherman and the Genie' mentions Solomon when he is released. The word 'genie' comes from the Latin 'genius,' meaning spirit, but it refers directly to the Arabic jinn. When Arabian Nights stories were translated into French and English in the 1700s, jinn became 'genies,' and the idea of genies in bottles granting three wishes became popular in Western culture. However, traditional jinn stories are more complex—jinn have their own societies, families, and rules, and they do not simply grant wishes. The fisherman's genie is closer to the original idea: powerful, dangerous, and bound by ancient magic that only the wise can navigate.

47.opinion: The genie's bitterness grew over 300 years in the jar. Can you understand why he became angry, even though it wasn't the fisherman's fault?

grammar.example: The genie roared, 'Solomon! I repent! I repent!'

48.title: The Three Princes and Princess Nouronnihar

vocabulary.definition: polite, kind, and pleasant
vocabulary.sentence: She was the most intelligent and gracious young woman in the kingdom.

vocabulary.definition: brought back to health or to a normal condition
vocabulary.sentence: Any person who smelled it, however ill, however close to death, would be immediately restored to full health.

vocabulary.definition: deep, careful thought
vocabulary.sentence: Houssain retreated to the hills and spent his life in quiet contemplation, which suited him.

comprehension: What did the Sultan ask his three sons to do?
What magical item did each prince bring back?
How did all three princes work together to save Princess Nouronnihar?
Why did the princess choose Prince Ahmed?
48.journal: Which prince showed the most wisdom: the one with the carpet, the tube, or the apple? Explain your answer.

informational.title: Fairness and Difficult Choices
informational.part1: How do you make a fair decision when more than one answer seems right? The Sultan in 'The Three Princes' faced this exact problem when all three of his sons helped save Princess Nouronnihar's life. Without the magic carpet, they could not have arrived in time. Without the seeing tube, they would not have known she was ill. Without the healing apple, arriving quickly would have meant nothing. Each contribution was essential, and each brother deserved recognition. In real life, parents, teachers, leaders, and judges often face similar situations where multiple people contribute to a success, or where several solutions seem equally valid. Deciding what is fair requires careful thought about what each person gave and what the situation truly needs.
informational.part2: Sometimes the fairest answer is to reward people differently based on what they sacrificed. The Sultan noticed that the brothers who kept their magical objects still had them—they had not given up anything permanent. Ahmed, however, used his healing apple and could never use it again. He gave up everything he had brought back. The princess recognized this difference and made her choice. In other situations, fairness might mean sharing the reward equally, or finding a creative solution so everyone benefits, or letting the person most affected by the decision have a voice in making it. Fair does not always mean equal or the same. It means considering what each person contributed, what they gave up, and what the outcome requires. The hardest decisions are not between right and wrong, but between two things that are both partly right.

48.opinion: Was it fair for the princess to choose Ahmed because he gave up his treasure? Write your opinion with reasons.

grammar.example: When the cap finally came free, thick smoke poured out.
